title: The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.
caption:
The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita, 2023

DALYs per 100,000 (DALYs per 100,000 people)

Across countries in 2023, the burden of disease measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years per 100,000 individuals shows a clear negative relationship with GDP per capita plotted on a logarithmic axis. The vertical axis spans from 0 to 80,000 DALYs, while the horizontal axis ranges from $2,000 to $100,000 in international dollars at 2021 prices. Countries in Africa, shown in purple, cluster in the upper-left quadrant with high disease burden and low income: the Central African Republic reaches approximately 80,000 DALYs, Niger and Chad sit near 70,000, while Burundi, DR Congo, and Mozambique fall between 55,000 and 65,000. Asian countries in teal occupy the middle range, with India and Pakistan around 40,000-45,000 DALYs at $5,000-$7,000 GDP, while China drops to roughly 20,000 DALYs at $20,000 GDP. European nations in blue appear in the lower-right, with Poland, Germany, and Japan all below 25,000 DALYs at GDP levels above $40,000. The United States sits near 32,000 DALYs at approximately $60,000 GDP, positioned higher than other high-income countries. Circle sizes indicate population, with India and China shown as the largest circles. Greenland appears as an outlier with roughly 40,000 DALYs despite GDP near $80,000. The data excludes international aviation. GDP per capita is measured in international dollars at 2021 prices to account for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. Higher GDP per capita coincides with lower disease burden across the visible range.
